IMPACT.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—representing multiword concepts and linking diverse surface forms to canonical concepts—by jointly embedding concepts, phrases, and words using ontology mappings (UMLS, YAGO) and unannotated corpora (it “requires no human annotations”). Its results are convincing enough to change practice: the concept embeddings are “competitive with prior models that required human annotations” while providing “more than 3x coverage in the vocabulary size,” and the authors also release a novel real‑world entity similarity dataset. The approach is broadly applicable (biomedical and general domains) and suggests clear next steps (e.g., incorporating ontology structure), so it is likely to be adopted and to open useful follow‑on work.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: Yes — the paper is squarely within NLP/ML: it proposes a novel representation-learning method (jointly embedding concepts, phrases, and words) built on standard ML techniques (skip-gram with negative sampling) and distant supervision from ontologies (Section 3.2). The evaluations follow community norms (intrinsic Spearman correlations on MayoSRS/UMNSRS and a new real-entity dataset, comparisons to prior concept-embedding work such as De Vine, Choi, and Mencia in Section 5), and the related-work framing cites appropriate NLP/ML literature (knowledge-base and embedding methods). Overall the problem formulation, methods, benchmarks, and positioning are appropriate for a top-tier NLP/ML venue.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes an incremental but useful idea: adapting skip-gram/negative-sampling to jointly learn EW, EP and EC by using ontology-linked representative phrases as distant supervision (Sections 3.2, equations 2–4) rather than requiring manual concept annotation. The approach is clearly differentiated from prior CUI- or code-based methods (De Vine 2014, Choi 2016, Mencia 2016) and from pure knowledge-graph embedding work by relying on text contexts of phrase forms, but it mainly combines existing techniques (word2vec training, negative sampling) with ontology-derived weak labels. Overall, the technical changes are a non-trivial engineering/algorithmic extension but represent moderate novelty rather than a fundamentally new paradigm.

CLARITY.justification: The paper has a generally logical high-level organization (Introduction → Method → Materials → Experiments), and the core model and objective are presented with equations (Sections 3.2–3.2.1). However, the provided text contains many duplicated and garbled paragraphs (repeated blocks in Sections 1–5), missing or inconsistently referenced figures/tables (Figure 1/2/3/4 and Table 1 are referenced but not shown or incomplete), and occasional underspecified notation (some variables and sets are used before full clarification). These presentation and formatting problems make key parts hard to follow and require substantial cleanup for clear reading.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The paper compares against several appropriate and recent biomedical baselines (De Vine et al. 2014, Choi et al. 2016, Mencia et al. 2016, Chiu et al. 2016) and evaluates on standard datasets (MayoSRS, UMNSRS) using Spearman’s ρ. Comparisons appear fair (same evaluation metrics, discussion of corpus choices and hyperparameters) and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods (e.g., CHOI) outperform theirs and analyze differences (compositionality, MAP, clustering). One minor omission is limited direct comparison to knowledge-graph/entity embedding methods for the general-domain YAGO/entity dataset despite citing that literature, so the comparisons are strong in the biomedical setting but not fully comprehensive across domains.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper runs a broad set of experiments across multiple corpora (PUBMED, CLINICAL, PM+CL, WIKINYT), evaluates on established biomedical benchmarks (MayoSRS, UMNSRS) and new real‑world entity data, and compares against several prior embedding methods (DeVine, Choi, Mencia, Chiu) while providing analyses (cosine/MAP, clustering by semantic type). It includes useful ablation-style comparisons (APPROXPHRco/APPROXWORD/PHRASEco/WORD) and discusses limitations (e.g. missing non‑lexical information, hyperparameter generalization issues). However, sensitivity analyses are limited (no systematic hyperparameter sweeps reported beyond corpus variants), and reproduction details are incomplete (training hyperparameters, negative‑sampling/epoch/learning‑rate specifics and exact tagging procedure are not fully specified). These gaps prevent an “extremely thorough” rating but overall the empirical evaluation is solid.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper clearly states a joint embedding method (Sec. 3.2) with a simple, correct adaptation of skip-gram with negative sampling (Eqs. 1–4) and provides empirical evaluations on multiple corpora and datasets (UMLS/YAGO; Secs. 4–5) comparing against reasonable baselines (De Vine, Choi, Mencia, CHIU). However, the distant-supervision assumption that every occurrence of a known phrase indicates all concepts it can refer to is strong and untreated (Intro), and the paper does not report statistical significance or full hyperparameter details despite noting their importance (Sec. 2 Hyperparameter tuning), which leaves some methodological gaps regarding robustness to ambiguous phrases. These are minor issues that do not undermine the main experimental claims but warrant further analysis.
